oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Reduced feed efficiency, pathologic cardiopulmonary remodeling, and increased mortality in feedyard cattle with underlying pulmonary hypertension
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Krafsur, G.M.
Culbertson, M.M.
Brown, R.D.
Holt, T.N.
Enns, R.M.
Bowen, R.A.
Stenmark, K.R.
Thomas, M.G.
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Heart failure (HF) increasingly impacts animal health, perfor­mance and profitability in moderate elevation feedlots. Pulmo­nary hypertension (PH) induced by hypoxic environment is a risk factor for right heart failure (RHF) and death in cattle at high elevation. We hypothesized that a subset of susceptible al­titude-naïve cattle would exhibit pre-existing or early-onset PH and pathologic cardiopulmonary remodeling, reduced feedlot performance characteristics, and increased mortality during fattening at moderate elevation.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 246
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
